data.description:

The abrdn Global Infrastructure Fund (AIAFX) outperformed its benchmark in the fourth quarter of 2025. 1

The performance data quoted represents past performance and current returns may be lower or higher. Class A shares have up to a 5.75% front-end sales charge and a 0.25% 12b-1 fee.

The investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. To obtain performance information current to the most recent month-end, which may be higher or lower than the performance shown above, please call 866-667-9231 or go to aberdeeninvestments.com/us/literature.

Returns prior to May 7, 2018 reflect the performance of a predecessor fund. Please consult the Fund’s prospectus for more detail.

Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions.

Total returns may reflect a waiver of part of the Fund’s fees for certain periods since inception, without which returns would have been lower. Indexes are unmanaged and provided for comparison purposes only. No fees or expenses are reflected. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Expense ratios (%)

Class A

Institutional Class

1.59/1.25

1.33/1.00

Expenses stated as of the Fund’s most recent prospectus. All classes of the Fund have contractual waivers in place and may not be terminated before February 28, 2026 without approval of the Independent Trustees.

In terms of individual stock positive contributors, Loma Negra Compania Industrial Argentina (LOMA), the Argentinian cement company, performed strongly after Milei won sufficient votes to continue his reform agenda, having previously sold off in the months leading up to the Argentinian elections amid concerns that he was losing support.

SSE (SSEZY), the UK electric utility, announced an updated capital expenditure (capex) and funding plan that surprised positively. The company raised £2 billion in equity, less than expected, and outlined a £33 billion capex plan through to 2030, higher than expected, which is expected to support 7–9% per annum earnings growth.

RWE (RWEOY), the European electric utility, rose over the quarter as investors grew more optimistic about an AI-driven power market recovery in Europe, similar to what has been seen in the US over the past 18 months. This was supported by greater visibility on planned investment in hyperscale data centres across Europe.

In terms of individual stock detractors, Infrastrutture Wireless Italiane (IFSUF), the Italian tower company, reported disappointing earnings that resulted in a cut to long-term earnings guidance only eight months after it was issued, with management citing reduced visibility from its key customers. One possible driver is a reluctance among customers to commit amid the potential for telecommunications sector consolidation in Italy.

Rumo (RUMOF), the Brazilian rail operator, continued to see weak pricing despite decent volumes, as it faced near-term competitive pricing pressure in some of its key transportation corridors. Separately, its share price was pressured by its largest shareholder, Cosan, selling down two separate 5% tranches of its holding. This reflected liquidity needs at Cosan rather than any fundamental issues at Rumo.

American Tower Corporation (AMT), the US tower company, underperformed as investors reacted negatively to news that EchoStar indicated it would not honour the remainder of its contract with American Tower after the sale of its spectrum. American Tower is appealing the decision, but this is likely to be a protracted legal dispute. For context, the EchoStar contracts represent around 2% of American Tower’s revenues.

Market review

Global equities ended the final quarter of 2025 higher, supported by strong corporate earnings and a couple of 25-basis-point rate cuts from the US Federal Reserve (Fed) over the period. However, sentiment was constrained early in the period by the partial US government shutdown, which delayed key economic data and added to policy uncertainty before a temporary funding bill was agreed in mid-November to run into January 2026. Softer US labour market data and signs of easing inflation later strengthened investor conviction that the Fed could implement further interest-rate cuts in the coming months. Meanwhile, technology companies continued to advance due to optimism about artificial intelligence (AI) despite some volatility in November linked to concerns about valuations in the sector. However, persistent geopolitical tensions surrounding the Russia–Ukraine war and mixed Chinese economic data continued to cloud the global growth outlook.

Outlook & strategy

The focus within energy remains on high-quality midstream businesses with long-term take-or-pay contracts, particularly those exposed to gas. The outlook for gas infrastructure has improved as demand has strengthened, especially in the US, where gas is increasingly important in meeting power needs from AI-related data centres. New commercial opportunities, including integrated power solutions for hyperscalers, are reinforcing the role of gas within the broader energy mix.

In transport infrastructure, roads continue to perform well globally, with traffic volumes generally above pre-pandemic levels and supportive pricing dynamics in the US and Canada. Emerging markets offer growth opportunities, particularly where further privatisation is expected, such as in Brazil. The political environment in France remains a concern, with higher taxation weighing on toll road operators and leading to reduced exposure. Airports remain an area of focus, especially those with a strong leisure travel bias, with opportunities across emerging markets including Argentina, Mexico and Thailand. Rail volumes remain weak, although operational performance in the US has improved.

Within utilities, rising power demand linked to AI remains a central theme, with emphasis on regulated and contracted parts of the sector. In the US, higher capital expenditure is shaping long-term growth expectations, while increased power purchase agreement prices have supported renewable developers. Regulatory dynamics and pressure on customer bills remain key considerations.

In towers, emerging-market exposure performed strongly, reflecting improving financial performance and increasing investor recognition. While some positions have been reduced for risk management reasons, the outlook for African tower operators remains constructive. Developed-market towers continue to face headwinds from higher interest rates and slower telecommunications capital expenditure, with uncertainty persisting in Europe due to consolidation and excess capacity.

Overall, infrastructure continues to stand out as an attractive investment opportunity, providing defensive characteristics and long-term value in an uncertain macroeconomic environment. We also believe infrastructure offers a compelling way to navigate volatility while benefiting from essential, irreplaceable assets. With the focus on near-term trends and political developments, it is important not to lose sight of the long-term structural drivers in infrastructure, namely the energy transition, digital acceleration, ageing infrastructure and increasing urbanisation.

1 The S&P Global Infrastructure Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of stock markets of developed and emerging markets.

For current holdings information, please visit abrdn Global Infrastructure Fund - Portfolio Holdings

Important information

The fund invests in emerging market equities and / or bonds. Investing in emerging markets involves a greater risk of loss than investing in more developed markets due to, among other factors, greater political, tax, economic, foreign exchange, liquidity and regulatory risks.

The Fund is subject to the risk of concentrating investments in infrastructure-related companies, which makes it more susceptible to factors adversely affecting issuers within that industry than would a fund investing in a more diversified portfolio of securities. data.title: Baidu's AI Initiatives Are Exciting, What About Search And The Rest?
data.description:
Thesis

Baidu, Inc. (BIDU) is a hold in my opinion. Since 2H25, the stock has surged significantly, given the narrative of AI hardware reshoring. Domestic fabless designers like Cambricon and Moore Threads have done exceptionally well, backed by sales growth in 2024 and 2025. Baidu also has its initiative, Kunlunxin, which is in the process of an IPO, in addition to consistent traction of its Robotaxi unit, Apollo Go. Arguably, Kunlunxin has been the main driver of stock performance. I think the market is quite bullish on that, potentially valuing the unit at over $10 billion vs. the implied $3 billion (going to be great for those subscribers). That said, the current sentiment on AI is wobbly while emotionally high for China's domestic players. In addition, Baidu has a large chunk of legacy businesses that bring in more than 50% of earnings. The (structural) decline of those is, in my opinion, an overlooked fundamental driver of the business.

Performance

About the company

Baidu is a major big-tech company in China, along with Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) and Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY). Think of this business like the Google of China, where new initiatives like AI were built on top of its search business with over 700 million MAUs. That said, search is overall counterproductive to growth given its structural and gradual decline; therefore, it is dubbed a "legacy" business with a stagnating MAU and declining ARPU.

To combat this, Baidu has been pursuing growth initiatives by stepping on the gas for AI. Many of these bets have a real trajectory for significant value creation. We can see through outcomes like Apollo Go (its robotaxi service), which is arguably the leader in the intelligence of autonomous driving vehicles in China, its upcoming IPO, its AI chip unit Kunlunxin, and the significant growth of AI infrastructure and application in FY25. AI intelligence is the key bet for differentiation, with arguably a more comprehensive suite.

Trajectory of Apollo Go

Legacy businesses

Legacy businesses like its search/advertisement and iQIYI streaming are facing stagnation. Management has mentioned that since 1q24, growth in AI-native marketing revenue has reached CNY2.8 billion in 3Q25, up 262% YoY. This may seem impressive. However, overall online marketing revenue was CNY16.6 billion for the same quarter, while monthly ARPU had slipped to CNY7.8.

The role of integrating AI hasn't been to spearhead new opportunities for growth but rather to slow down the decline of a business line where its competitiveness has been eroding. ARPU is pretty much the only metric available to illustrate this objectively. In my opinion, living in China for over a decade, search is really not a prominent source of information. People would prefer access to information from sources like WeChat and Xiaohongshu, a thriving app with over 300 million MAU. Here is an interesting take on the topic.

The other legacy business is iQIYI, Inc. (IQ), which Baidu owns 53% of and is valued at $1.9 billion with a CNY6.6 billion quarterly revenue for 3Q25. This is also a troublesome segment with declining users and profits that turned negative since 3Q25. The company has been relying on growth through international markets like SEA and LATAM, which may have driven up the sales and administrative expenses while having to compete with established players like Netflix.

Moving forward, iQIYI is betting on better ad monetization through AI, just like every consumer tech company in China, and also potentially monetization of following Popmart's footprint of IP monetization. I think both are challenging. Ad monetization is already over CNY 10 per month against the subscribing user base. AI-driven content generation is something with low barriers to entry, and saturation will likely come fast. As for IP-driven earnings, the results are uncertain given the creative nature and lack of timeliness as opposed to a first-mover like Popmart. I am not counting too much on this segment to contribute much to Baidu's bottom line while noting its mere $1.9 billion valuation.

Valuation and risks

Baidu trades mostly at par with Chinese tech peers. The likes of Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) and Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY) operate with strong moats as large-scale players that dominate in e-commerce, social media, and gaming. It is not the same for Baidu, where valuation is driven by the narrative of China's chip stack on-shoring, in sync with firms like Cambricon. These are aggressive stories expecting high double-digit growth in the next 3-5 years and even beyond.

Onshoring by domestic chipmakers: outsized gains

For Baidu, the potential IPO for Kunlunxin is speculated to be valued at $3 billion, a 3x P/S multiple for FY26's projected sales. That said, I do believe that the market is currently pricing a 10x P/S multiple. With Baidu's 59% stake, it is contributing $5.9 billion of Baidu's total market capitalization. For reference, Cambricon's fwd P/S is at about 40x predicated upon sustained domestic and even international growth.

This is a big growth bet and one that rests on the assumptions that 1) R&D progress of the chips themselves will be increasingly competitive against US alternatives, 2) scaling laws are still very much in place with data centers continuing to sustain their capex spending, and 3) China's chip manufacturers (namely SMIC) will keep up with output and improvement in lithography processes.

Final take

Baidu's recent narratives focused much on its AI strengths and speculative measures of its 59% stake in Kunlunxin, which is in the process of an IPO. We've dissected the drivers of growth for Baidu. For the most part, the business is still facing stagnation, particularly from its bread-and-butter, search and ads, which represent over 50% of revenue and a bigger portion of income given higher margins. To that end, I am skeptical that its operating income would grow in FY26, considering that ad profitability has been weakening quarter by quarter. Apparently, this is different from the consensus.

Quarterly metrics

In recent weeks, Baidu has experienced some pullback amid a price correction of global tech stocks. The repricing doesn't have much to do with fundamentals, as earnings stayed robust. However, it has more to do with AI eroding the moat of SaaS businesses. Perhaps the progress of AI has surprised to the upside and thus changed the outlook of hardware demand. Baidu's chip narrative is still more about reshoring amid geopolitical risks. That is arguably the dominant short-term driver of performance, one where optimism is still levelled.

data.title: Mixed U.K. Inflation Data No Gamechanger For The Bank of England
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

The latest UK inflation read is a mixed bag for the Bank of England, but we doubt it drastically changes the odds of a March rate cut.

Headline inflation is down from 3.4% to 3.0% in January, largely as expected. It's a consequence of a seasonal fall in airfares, lower fuel prices and the impact of last year's private school VAT change dropping out.

Most importantly, though, food inflation is down sharply – from 4.5% to 3.6%. That’s roughly inline with BoE forecasts, but it should help the hawks become a little more relaxed about the upside risks to inflation. A big concern last year was that higher food inflation would spark a much wider and more persistent bout of price pressure. Those concerns now look overblown.

But services inflation was stickier than expected in January. Importantly, that’s not really because of quirks like airfares or holidays. In fact, we calculate that the Bank's preferred measure of 'core services' inflation nudged up from 4.0% to 4.3% once volatile and indexed items are excluded. Catering – often seen as the archetypal service-sector category, one that’s driven by underlying economic demand – has nudged a little higher over the past couple of months.

Services inflation is proving sticky

Nothing here is a game-changer for the Bank. The views of doves and hawks alike are being driven by bigger-picture, structural thinking right now. One inflation report isn’t going to shift those views dramatically. Weakness in the jobs market, coupled with falling wage growth, is probably a more important consideration right now.

The bigger test comes in April. We still think inflation will temporarily dip below target to 1.9% before settling around 2% through the summer. That’s down to a whole raft of things, but notably lower energy costs and less aggressive water bill increases. If we’re right, that should go some way to making the committee as a whole more relaxed about the inflation outlook.

We continue to expect rate cuts in March and June – and we don’t rule out more.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Whale's Methodology: Investing Through The Lens Of Volatility (II)
data.description:
Across most asset classes, historical data show a strong association between high volatility and falling prices. Equities, credit, FX and crypto alike tend to experience non-linear volatility spikes when risk appetite suddenly contracts and prices sell off, typically alongside a marked deterioration in liquidity. While this is often framed as a simple rise in risk aversion, a more informative explanation lies in the interaction between investor behaviour and market microstructure: inflows during uptrends are usually more dispersed and incremental, whereas selling during downturns is more synchronised and more urgent, making "liquidity cliffs" more likely.

In recent years, however, changes in market structure have weakened the idea that “high volatility belongs to down markets”. Greater penetration of derivatives and leveraged instruments has made sharp volatility increasingly plausible during rallies as well. The underlying mechanism is that leverage stresses liquidity without an inherent directional preference: when trends are persistent, positioning becomes crowded and hedging chains lengthen, liquidity can be depleted quickly in either direction, leading to secondary volatility surges and discontinuous price moves. This shifts the centre of gravity in tail-risk management from a traditional, predominantly downside focus to a framework that explicitly addresses both upside and downside tails, with volatility and liquidity treated as primary state variables.

Why Volatility Clusters in Sell-Offs

The asymmetry starts with trading behaviour. In rising markets, participants more often build exposure gradually and hold it. Demand tends to be spread over time—through staggered accumulation, buying on pullbacks and short covering—so flows look more like a steady stream than a single shock. Risk-bearing capacity also tends to be healthier in uptrends; market makers quote more confidently, spreads are tighter, and order books typically absorb incremental flow with lower impact costs.

During downturns, selling becomes both more synchronous and more time-sensitive. Stop-losses, risk limits, margin pressures, forced redemptions and de-risking mandates frequently trigger around similar price levels, creating “clustered” order arrival. When concentrated selling hits, depth is consumed quickly; at the same time, market makers widen spreads or step back as volatility and inventory risk rise, causing effective liquidity to be “squeezed out”. In that regime, prices are more likely to gap as markets clear, and realised and implied volatility rise together.

This microstructural pattern is consistent with the volatility skew observed in traditional options markets: out-of-the-money puts tend to trade at higher implied volatilities, reflecting the historical frequency and speed of downside tail events and their reliance on scarce liquidity. For a long time, this anchored risk management around left-tail protection and drawdown control.

Leverage and Derivatives: Volatility Becomes Direction-Agnostic

Classical risk frameworks are increasingly strained by today’s instruments and market plumbing. The widespread use of perpetual swaps, futures and options, alongside leveraged ETFs, margin trading and structured products, has strengthened a feedback loop:

Position build-up → margin and risk-budget dynamics → liquidations/rebalancing → price impact → higher volatility → tighter margin and risk constraints.

Crucially, this loop does not inherently favour either direction; it is highly sensitive to crowded positioning and synchronised rebalancing.

In rising markets, leverage can amplify volatility through two channels. The first is trend-following and leverage accumulation: mark-to-market gains reduce effective margin strain and free up risk budget, encouraging further position increases and creating positive feedback. The second is hedging flow that becomes pro-cyclical: when markets carry substantial call options or structured upside exposure, hedgers seeking to maintain risk neutrality may need to buy spot or futures as prices rise, reinforcing “accelerating” phases of rallies.

In falling markets, the amplification channels are more familiar. Margin shortfalls trigger forced deleveraging and liquidations; leveraged ETFs can generate persistent sell pressure through intraday and close-to-close rebalancing; and, in negative-gamma conditions, options hedging can become trend-following, pushing additional selling into declines and further lifting volatility.

Once leverage can drain liquidity on both sides, tail risk is no longer a purely downside phenomenon. In modern market structure, “risk” increasingly resembles the interaction between volatility and liquidity rather than a simple directional exposure. Portfolio objectives, therefore, extend beyond “avoiding drawdowns” to maintaining a controlled P&L distribution and an executable rebalancing plan through two-sided volatility spikes.

Options in Portfolio Volatility Management: Cost Discipline Matters

Options offer convex, non-linear pay-offs in exchange for a known premium, making them a natural tool for tail protection and for managing portfolio volatility when liquidity shocks and volatility spikes propagate. Options can serve both downside protection and mitigation of upside squeeze risk—for example, where structural under-allocation to risk assets or residual short exposure creates vulnerability to sharp rallies—by acquiring limited-cost upside convexity.

The practical constraint is cost. Systematic protection typically implies persistent premium outlay and negative carry. Without a disciplined budget, hedging programmes are often curtailed during quiet periods (precisely when they appear least necessary) and then absent when protection is most valuable. A more institutional approach treats option premia explicitly as insurance costs and assigns them a stable, explainable funding source.

Fixed-income allocations (such as government bonds) and fixed-income-style strategies (such as carry trades, curve roll-down and selected credit carry) can provide relatively predictable premia in many “normal” regimes and may be used—fully or partially—to fund option premia. This can turn tail protection from an ad hoc purchase into an embedded portfolio mechanism. That said, fixed income is not tail-free: inflation shocks, rapid rate rises and liquidity squeezes can generate meaningful drawdowns, and carry strategies can unwind sharply in risk events. Any “carry funds insurance” design therefore benefits from stress testing for correlation regime shifts and liquidity constraints to reduce the risk of compounding losses in extreme conditions.

Volatility-Aware Opportunities in Stressed Markets

A defining feature of high-volatility regimes is the widening of mispricings across assets, maturities and instruments. The distortions are not limited to “price versus fair value”; they also show up as term structure dislocations, gaps between implied and realised volatility, widening cross-venue basis, and breakdowns in correlation structure. The common driver is that, when liquidity is scarce, prices are set primarily by urgent flows rather than by valuation anchors.

Term Structure as a Liquidity Gauge

In drawdown-driven high-volatility phases, the central challenge is often not deciding whether assets are “cheap”, but whether liquidity has started to heal. If liquidation cascades remain active, forced selling persists, and market depth is still fragile, prices can continue to gap lower. Relative to using price rebounds alone as a signal, the implied-volatility term structure can more directly reflect near-term hedging demand and funding stress.

A practical monitoring framework is to track transitions from backwardation to contango in the volatility term structure. When short-DTEs implied volatility trades well above longer-DTEs levels, markets are typically in an acute stress state. As front-end implied volatility falls and the curve normalises into a smoother, upward-sloping structure, it often coincides with easing forced-flow pressure and the repair of marginal liquidity. Deploying capital in stages around that inflection can be more robust than relying on price-based timing.

In both crypto and traditional markets, platforms such as Optionscharts and Amberdata can be used to monitor volatility term structure. Execution is generally more robust when term structure signals are complemented by spot-liquidity indicators—bid–ask spreads, order-book depth—and by funding or basis measures, helping to reduce the probability of stepping in before liquidity is sufficiently restored.

Correlation Breaks and Relative-Value Trades

During volatility upshifts and deleveraging, correlation structures frequently become unstable. Assets that are usually highly correlated can decouple due to differences in liquidity, leverage distribution, and positioning crowding; assets that are generally weakly correlated can briefly move in lockstep through systemic risk transmission. For short-horizon and relative-value strategies, such “mismatches” can push spreads and ratios away from historical ranges, creating conditions for mean reversion once liquidity stabilises.

Pair trading can be structurally attractive in high-volatility regimes because it can hedge directional risk and concentrate exposure on spread convergence and correlation normalisation. The trade-off is that spread volatility rises materially, and correlation breakdowns can persist longer than expected, increasing the importance of position sizing, stop disciplines and liquidity constraints. A more institutional approach selects pairs based on explainable drivers—financing costs, basis structure, rebalancing mechanics, options-hedging pressure, and supply–demand or liquidity asymmetries—and monitors microstructure variables such as funding rates, borrow costs, depth and order-book resilience, rather than relying solely on statistical correlation.

A Portfolio Framework Centred on Liquidity and Volatility

The preceding analysis points to a consistent conclusion: with broader use of derivatives and leverage, volatility spikes can occur on either side of a trend, requiring a shift from one-sided “downside-only” hedging to a two-sided tail-risk framework. Options remain central for managing convexity and volatility exposure, but sustainability depends on explicit premium budgeting and credible funding sources; fixed income and carry can contribute, but must be assessed under extreme correlation and liquidity scenarios. In terms of opportunities, volatility term structure can help map the pace of liquidity repair for longer-horizon allocations. At the same time, correlation breaks can create relative-value windows for shorter-horizon strategies.

Overall, volatility is increasingly more than a passive by-product of stress; it has become a key indicator of market structure, leverage feedback and liquidity conditions. Introducing a volatility-aware lens can therefore improve both portfolio resilience and the quality of opportunity identification in two-sided tail environments.

The discussion above focuses on using volatility logic to shape portfolio risk controls and to identify potential entry points and relative-value opportunities during high-volatility regimes. The next instalment will move to volatility trading in the strict sense—treating volatility itself as the tradable underlier. That requires a framework for pricing and trading implied versus realised volatility, and structuring cross-tenor and cross-underlier relative-volatility trades—alongside execution and risk controls under liquidity shocks and correlation regime shifts. Stay tuned!

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

data.title: Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile Is Best Positioned To Lead As Lithium Pricing Rebounds
data.description:
Company Background

Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM), founded in 1968 and based in Santiago, Chile, is one of the world’s leading producers of lithium, with an estimated 17-18% global market share. Outside of lithium, they also have large mining operations in iodine, specialty plant nutrient (SPN), potassium, and industrial chemicals. Excepting potassium, they have a strong share in each of these respective markets.

In the TTM period, their gross profit split was as follows, with 84% shared between lithium and iodine. Lithium is considered their most important growth driver, considering iodine pricing—as I’ll discuss in more detail below—being much more stable and predictable; a slow-growing but consistent revenue-generating segment, but not one that is expected to see more than a modest demand increase going forward.

SQM’s main operations are in Chile, from where it generates nearly all of its revenue, as seen from the map below. Chile has the largest lithium reserves in the world, even surpassing Australia, which currently produces more lithium than Chile. This suggests that Chile could regain its leading position in the industry.

Minerals tend to be highly cyclical, with supply and demand economics playing the major role in determining sales pricing and sales volume, the two key metrics underlying the revenues accrued by companies like SQM. A recent global oversupply of lithium hurt their revenues over the past few years, heading into 2025, which is why their share price fell markedly during this period. This cyclicality can be seen in their stock chart below.

We can see the convergence of SQM’s stock price with lithium pricing here, just by crudely eyeballing it.

One thing we notice is that the share price has drifted higher relative to the price of lithium, and this is because investors always look ahead, in most cases 6-12 months, sometimes more in ultra-bullish environments, and often less in bearish ones. I discussed the typical bullish/bearish swings in sentiment in my last article.

Previous Coverage

I first covered SQM in December of 2024, in one of my first articles since becoming a Seeking Alpha analyst. It was a company that I had owned for a few years and recently doubled down on, yet at the time shares had hit a multi-year low (around $38). In my article, I highlighted historically low lithium prices as the key reason for the share price collapse, alongside recent news that suggested efforts by the Chilean government to nationalize the industry. These were threats that, taken together, were likely more of a short-term sentiment-killer than a long-term thesis-destroyer, in my opinion.

Weighing up these risks with the opportunities presented by a likely rebound in lithium prices, due especially to the ongoing global EV roll-out, I issued a “buy” rating on the stock and suggested patience. Since then, as I expected, SQM shares have increased nicely, up ~85% in less than 14 months. Much of this jump came in the last two months of 2025, and around the turn of the new year, 2026, shares reached a high of over $85 (representing a 123% gain if anyone managed to time it right). They have since pulled back to around $71.

Much of this performance was due to improving fundamentals in a highly cyclical industry, for sure, but there has also been a good deal of hype attached to precious metals generally as geopolitical disputes and threats have spilled into the market landscape. These issues have been discussed at length elsewhere, notably Trump’s response to escalating trade tensions with China related to rare earths and Trump’s threats on Greenland’s sovereignty amid rare earths being politicized.

The result has been an improvement in sentiment across the entire space over the past 12 months, but it is seen most strikingly in the spiking prices of spot gold and silver. Lithium and other important metals used in industry and technology, like copper, have also seen, in some cases, explosive increases in spot pricing, with companies in these spaces enjoying market outperformance.

Like SQM’s management (and many others), investors are anticipating the supply-demand economics to continue shifting, resulting in lithium prices continuing to increase, which would be bullish for SQM.

In this article, I’ll assess the supply-demand economics of lithium and where SQM is positioned in this development, alongside what its competitive advantages are in the industry, before turning to its own financials and valuation metrics to ascertain the risk-reward profile of the company at its current share price around $71.

Lithium: SQM’s Main Growth Driver

In lithium, SQM has enjoyed modest increases in sales volume over the past few years, but with lithium prices bottoming out—falling from a high in 2022—the metal has come to represent a smaller proportion of their revenue and gross profit (now just 40%).

In lithium, they form a kind of global oligopoly alongside Albemarle (ALB) and Rio Tinto (RIO)—which recently purchased Arcadium—alongside a couple of Australian firms (Mineral Resources Ltd. and PLS Group) and two other Chinese firms, Ganfeng Lithium Group (GNENF) and Tianqi Lithium. The latter owns a large stake in SQM, dropping slightly to around 20% following a recent equity sale.

SQM produced around 225 kMT tons of lithium annually just from its mines in Chile (producing lithium hydroxide and lithium carbonate), but since Q1 2025, they have begun extracting lithium through their international division. This includes a mining operation in Sichuan, China (lithium hydroxide), and Mt. Holland, Australia (spodumene—lithium mineral deposits). They have a second operation in Australia, in Kwinana, which is run jointly (50/50) with Wesfarmers (WFAFY) to produce battery-grade lithium hydroxide (approx. 50 kMT per year), and an exploration site in Andover. Combined, TTM lithium production reached 238.9 kMT.

In the TTM period, SQM’s lithium sales volume increased by 43%, but year-on-year average sales prices fell by 15%, hurting their margins.

Lithium has been beset with oversupply problems, as I mentioned earlier. Over the last couple years, several companies have closed lithium mines to manage the oversupply. In July 2024, Core Lithium closed a mining project in Finniss, Australia, due to rising costs. This came after Albemarle scaled back its expansion plans at Kemerton earlier in the year, cancelling Trains 3 and 4. Similarly, Australian miner Mineral Resources placed its Bald Hill lithium mine on “care and maintenance” (essentially, shutting it down) in November 2024. The latter news was forecast to drop the production from an initial estimate of 120,000-145,000 dry metric tons (dmt) of spodumene to just 60,000 dmt. Other miners, including Australia’s Liontown Limited (LINRF) and the US-based Piedmont Lithium (PLLTL), also took steps in 2024 to scale back production to reduce costs due to falling lithium pricing. In China, government authorities recently announced that 27 mining permits were canceled in Jiangxi, the Chinese “lithium hub,” which aligns with the shuttering of mining activity in CATL's Jianxiawo lithium mine in August 2025 amid Beijing's anti-involution campaign.

All of this points to reduced supply, which will benefit those miners that continue to sustain supply as demand increases. SQM is one of them. While its competitors were cutting back, SQM held steady—maintaining production guidance—and adopted what Americas Quarterly called a “pedal-to-the-metal supply strategy.” In an interview, the SQM CEO Ricardo Ramos reasoned, ‘There’s no way the industry can survive these prices.’ Given their superior margins, the company has not shuttered their operations or reduced their capital spending plan for lithium production as much as some of their rivals, though they did reduce their 2025 capital spending plan by 31% to $1.1 billion.

Late last week—after scaling back production at its Kemerton site in 2024—Albemarle finally shut the mine altogether, citing weak economics (rising costs and competitive pressures in hard-rock lithium processing). However, they maintained that lithium prices should rebound and demand increase. They even provided the following graph in their most recent earnings call.

This highlights the broad challenges in terms of mining economics, but while SQM management shares the same view—of a tough current economic environment but very strong demand for lithium going forward (increasing 20% in 2025)—they have not shut their mines. They slightly reduced their workforce in June 2025, but this is not the same as shutting down a mine, which would then take months to bring back to life if the company decided to (as others had). The company’s diversity of operations—their revenue from iodine and other minerals—means that they were not forced to shut down anything. In the short term, there would certainly be a knock on their margins—which happened—but in the long term, their sustained operations in lithium have enabled them to take a larger proportion of the market (increasing their volume in light of declining selling prices) and be best positioned to enjoy any rebounds in spot pricing.

It stands to reason that the supply-demand balance will shift to SQM’s favour once supply and demand balance out. As seen below, they have industry-leading margins, especially compared to their closest rival—and the other major player in Chilean mining—Albemarle. Compared to Rio Tinto, they have lower margins, but the majority of their revenue is derived from iron ore, with lithium a fairly small amount at present. Compared to the two Chinese companies, SQM has a positive net margin. SQM’s margins have not contracted to the same extent as Tianqi and are generally much healthier than Ganfeng. Compared to Wesfarmers, they have an inferior gross margin but superior operating and net margins.

SQM also has a strong balance sheet. Of the six companies, they are the only one that has more cash (cash, equivalents, and short-term investments) than debt. Their cash position ($2.38 billion) is roughly 11.8% of their total market cap ($20.2 billion). Only Tianqi has a superior quick and current ratio.

Together, their strong margins and balance sheet have afforded them a competitive advantage in the industry. During the down market in lithium, it has enabled them to sustain their investment in this space instead of closing mines. This should further entrench the company as a leader in lithium production over the following years, as they will be able to capture a larger proportion of the market once the supply-demand imbalance shifts and the market rebounds. Their long-term stability trumps the short-term market fluctuations. Indeed, any company that can continue to invest, build infrastructure, expand production, and grow in a declining market will be better positioned to capitalize on a market rebound. That is happening now, and that—to my mind—is positive for the company and bullish for its stock price. So long as lithium pricing does not fall back to market lows of 2023/4, I see this set of conditions for the company as bullish. Let’s look at the forward demand for lithium.

Market Demand for Lithium

In almost every chart found online, experts predict increasing lithium demands going forward. This will mainly come from EVs but also from battery and energy storage systems (BESS), which SQM management anticipates will reach approximately 20% of global demand for the metal. In 2024, 87% of all lithium was for general battery use, according to this Canadian government website.

Given these secular tailwinds, I perceive this to be the start of a long-term rebound in lithium pricing, off the back of growing EV/BESS demand. In EVs, we especially have the Chinese to thank for this, who had longstanding EV subsidies to help spur demand. However, they have recently suggested scaling these back, but they have nevertheless set in motion a movement toward widespread EV adoption in the country that I see continuing. Indeed, they recently announced that they will double EV charging capacity to 180 gigawatts by 2027. Of course, ebbs and flows have been a constant feature of EV growth, but the long-term positive global trend is now clear.

In the last two years alone, where the growth rate was at 20%—despite Trump’s presidency and his less than warm stance toward the industry—we can see the upward trend and its seasonality. We can also see how dominant the Chinese market is (around 70% of sales) and the slowing of North American demand. I view the latter as temporary.

Interestingly, Canada introduced new EV subsidies to the industry; the Canadian government is now offering ‘up to $5,000 for battery-electric and fuel cell electric vehicles’ and ‘up to $2,500 for plug-in hybrid vehicles’ for EVs manufactured in countries that Canada has a free-trade agreement with. This should improve demand in North America, but of course it is in the US where the real growth runway lies outside of China. The global EV fleet now represents around 6-7%, so there is still a very long runway of growth ahead. I would argue that we are soon to reach what we might call a “tipping point” that should continue to push consumers to replace their diesel/petrol-fueled vehicles with EVs at a decent pace. Predictive models suggest very strong growth ahead as EVs increase as a proportion of all vehicles on the road.

Outside of EVs, the major development of data centres to spur the massive AI infrastructure build-out will also increase the demand for lithium as used in energy storage. Spending in this area is set to expand markedly over the coming years, as Amazon’s recent investment (capex spending) news highlights.

Taking all of this together, I believe the long-term trajectory of lithium demand remains strong and that SQM should stand to benefit.

Iodine and Other Minerals

In Iodine, where they are the world’s leading producer (37% market share), they expect very modest growth going forward, just 3% over the next 12 months, and basically flat volume growth. In the TTM period, they enjoyed a 5% increase in average sales prices, but sales volumes declined by 1%. To expand production and thereby grow market share in this more stable market segment, they have invested in constructing the Seawater Pipeline, which is expected to be finished within the next 6 months. The market for iodine is relatively stable, with market demand driven primarily by the x-ray contrast media segment. Iodine is used in radiography to enhance X-ray, CT, and fluoroscopic imaging, so it serves an important function in the biomedical industry.

In their general business operations, SQM enjoys industry-leading margins and capital allocation efficiency. This is due in part to their willingness and ability to sell the secondary materials (SPN, potassium, and industrial chemicals) that come as a byproduct from their primary lithium and iodine extraction operations. In the TTM period, these three secondary materials represented approximately 16% of their gross profit contribution. I view their diverse offerings as a strength, de-risking their operations and protecting them from market downturns across particular materials. Where other mining companies focused on just one material may have to shut down operations if the spot price of their main offering declines, SQM is not in that unenviable position. I view this as a strength that should come to command a slight premium over their competitors.

Risks

There are several risks to this bullish thesis. Firstly, their revenues are tied directly to lithium demand, and there is no guarantee that this will increase over the coming years. As stated above, rising numbers of EVs on the road and EV charging infrastructure being built, alongside new government subsidies, point to increasing demand, but this could change going forward, of course.

It is also possible that commercial preferences for lithium change. Indeed, several years ago, SQM recognized a global shift in specific lithium demand from lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃)—used in the production of Lithium Iron Phosphate lithium-ion batteries that have a lower energy density (used in solar panels and energy storage)—to lithium hydroxide (LiOH). The latter is used in the production of nickel, cobalt, and manganese (NMC)-based lithium-ion batteries that have a higher energy density and offer better performance. This makes them a favourite in EV production. While other companies enjoy a more focused mix in this regard, SQM, conversely, enjoys the capacity to shift their production between the two, given they can extract both from their mining operations in the Salar del Carmen region. Last year, they committed to increasing their mining capacity of LiOH from 40 kMT to 100 kMT.

A second risk worth mentioning is related to how the Chilean government might manage the company’s agreement with Codelco. We have had little news on this of late, and I do not see any clouds on the horizon in this regard. Nevertheless, it is something to keep an eye on. We are now 3 months after the recent Chile presidential elections, where José Antonio Kast won 58% of the vote. Analysts suggest this will likely cause a ‘recalibration rather than a rupture in Chile’s lithium policy, potentially reducing the degree of state involvement in the lithium industry that resulted from Boric’s state-centred National Lithium Strategy.’ Existing contracts with SQM are likely to be respected, but the government might also open up the industry to new private partnerships, adopting a more ‘market-oriented framework, with greater private participation through expertise and capital.’ I would agree with this analysis, so from my perspective, we are in a “wait and see” situation.

The lithium market is also cyclical, and we must anticipate that as the price of lithium ticks back up, as it has done recently, producers will reopen mines and supply will follow demand. It is important, therefore, for investors to take a long-term (or at least medium-term) view, as we are talking about the build-out of an entire industry that will not be linear but more likely lumpy. We have already seen early evidence of that as the macroeconomic and political winds shift direction.

Generally speaking, with its competitive advantages in scale, margins, balance sheet, and offerings mix—which allows them to withstand market downturns in one area by diversifying across different materials—I believe SQM is probably the best-positioned company to benefit from the upcoming increase in lithium demand. Let’s take a look at their valuation metrics to gauge risk/reward.

Valuation

SQM trades at an undemanding P/S and P/E valuation relative to its peers, as seen below.

Seeking Alpha currently ranks it 1 of 43 in the industry. Using Seeking Alpha’s statistics on valuation, they are slightly more expensive than Albemarle, but their forward growth looks better; hence, their lower forward 2- and 3-year PE ratios and superior PEG ratio.

Compared to itself historically, SQM is above its 5-year forward PE ratio due to its recent price appreciation but still far below its highs of 2021, when the market got ahead of itself. I view the current price as fairly valued and, with solid market growth going forward, consider the risk-reward quite favourable.

In terms of technicals, the chart looks good, forming a cup-and-handle pattern on the 3- and 5-year, which is bullish. The stock has continued to trend upwards, and, barring some unexpected news about lithium demand declining, I see more bullish indicators than bearish ones right now.

Conclusion

SQM enjoys market-leading margins and a strong balance sheet that has enabled it to withstand the current drawdown in lithium pricing, which has heavily impacted its revenues. I believe the demand for their highly prized lithium will continue to tick up, mainly as a consequence of increasing global EV adoption. As this meets the high (but falling) supply levels, due to other companies shuttering their mines, this will be bullish for SQM. This may take some time, but SQM has the benefit of having a diversified mix of materials for sale that should continue to see strong demand, sufficient to support the fluctuations in lithium pricing that we have seen, and likely will continue to see, going forward.

While I do expect this stock to remain quite volatile given some of the macroeconomic factors alongside geopolitical game-playing between world leaders, I continue to rate shares a buy. Investors should continue to exercise patience.

data.title: ACM Research: The Valuation Gap Is Shrinking But Still Wide Open
data.description:
It is always an interesting dynamic when a stock doubles in a matter of months and still looks cheap. Back in November, ACM Research (ACMR) was trading in the low $30s while printing fantastic growth numbers. The market was completely ignoring it due to a mix of geopolitical anxiety and a belief that Chinese semiconductor spending was due for a cyclical cliff.

Today the stock sits around $65. My read is that the market finally woke up to the clear arbitrage between the US-listed parent and the STAR Market-listed subsidiary. A lot of investors look at a 100% run in a single quarter and assume the easy money has been made, but I do not think that is the case here.

Even after the recent run, the valuation disconnect persists. The underlying fundamentals of the business are actually improving faster than the stock is re-rating. Management just dropped a very confident initial outlook for 2026, and the company is sitting on a mountain of cash following a significant capital raise in China. The market is still pricing ACMR like a cyclical hardware play that will eventually hit a wall of US export controls. The reality is they are building a premier platform company riding a state-sponsored domestic spending wave.

Forced Localisation

ACM Research makes wafer fabrication equipment (WFE). Historically they have dominated the domestic Chinese market for wafer cleaning tools. Cleaning is a great business to be in because it accounts for a huge chunk of front-end process steps. Every time process nodes shrink, contamination risks multiply, which means more cleaning steps are required.

The bear argument has always been that ACMR is a one-trick pony that is overly reliant on China. However, this ignores the fact that the Chinese government is subsidising a massive AI infrastructure buildout. Fabs there are buying domestic tools out of pure necessity to ensure survival against export controls, and ACMR benefits directly from this forced localisation.

What I really like is how they are using their dominance in cleaning to fund expansion into totally new product lines. Over the last year, they have rolled out new tools for electrochemical plating, thermal processing, and track systems. They even shipped their first Ultra Lith KrF track system to a Chinese logic fab back in September. They are methodically expanding their addressable market.

Not Slowing Down

When the company reported Q3-25 results back in November, revenue grew 32% YOY to $269m. There was some panic at the time because gross margins dipped to 42%, which is on the low end of their target model. But if you look closely at the mechanics of the business, this is normal. Management explicitly warned that gross margins will vary from period to period based on product mix and inventory provisions. They are heavily ramping up new product categories outside of core cleaning, and those new tools naturally carry slightly different margin profiles early in their lifecycle.

The most important update came in Jan. '26, where the company narrowed its 2025 revenue outlook to $885-900m but gave their initial 2026 guidance of $1.08-1.175bn. This counters the narrative that Chinese WFE spending is going through a digestion period.

Then there is the strong balance sheet. As of Sep-25, they had nearly $1.1bn in cash and time deposits. A massive chunk of that liquidity came from their principal operating subsidiary (ACM Shanghai), which raised roughly $623m in net proceeds on the STAR Market. This plus the cash generation from the cleaning business is being deployed to expand production capacity and push next-generation tool development.

Undervalued on Two Counts

Valuing this company requires looking past traditional US peer multiples. At the current price of around $65 and roughly 71m fully diluted shares, ACMR has a market cap of about $4.6bn.

ACMR owns 74.6% of its Shanghai-listed subsidiary (ACMS). ACMS currently trades at an equity value of roughly $12.7bn in China, which makes ACMR’s stake worth about $9.5bn. If we divide that $9.5bn stake by ACMR’s fully diluted share count, we get a per-share value of ~$133. That is before giving them any credit for the US parent's ex-China optionality or the cash pile.

Even if we just look at traditional multiples, the stock is still too cheap. Add roughly $287m in debt and subtract the $1.1bn in cash from the ~$4.6bn fully diluted market cap. That leaves an EV of around ~$3.8 billion. Against the midpoint of their 2026 revenue guidance of $1.12bn, the market is pricing a mere 3.4x forward EV/Sales. Compare that to legacy global players like Applied Materials (AMAT) or Lam Research (LRCX), which routinely trade at 9-13x forward sales.

If we apply a conservative 5x multiple to the midpoint of guidance ($1.12bn), we get a target EV of $5.6bn. Add back the net cash position (roughly $813m), and our target equity value is $6.41bn or ~$90 per share. So whether you look at it through the lens of its STAR Market subsidiary ($133 implied value) or a conservative peer multiple catch-up ($90 value), the stock has plenty of runway.

Paths to a Re-Rating

I see a few clear events that could force the market to close the valuation gap over the next year. First up is the FY25 earnings print due on Feb 26th. My expectation is that Q4 revenue will comfortably push them to the top end of their updated $885-900m guidance. But the topline isn't what the market is nervous about. It's the margins. After the gross margin dipped to 42% in Q3, I expect the upcoming numbers to show either a stabilisation or a slight recovery as the initial costs of ramping up new product categories begin to normalise.

I expect management will spend a lot of time talking up their platform expansion. I am looking for specific updates on repeat orders for their newly shipped Ultra Lith KrF track systems and progress at their Oregon R&D facility. These are important for their longer-term US expansion efforts. I also expect them to address the macro landscape, likely reiterating that Chinese fab spending is accelerating, driven by local capacity mandates.

How will the market react? If ACMR posts an EPS beat paired with reassuring commentary on the gross margin trajectory, my guess is that the remaining bears will be forced to cover. That kind of print should act as a clear catalyst to break the stock out of its current holding pattern. Alternatively, if margins stay compressed below 42%, the market might punish the stock short-term. Whatever happens, given their significant 2026 revenue visibility, a post-earnings dip would just create an even wider entry window for the arbitrage play.

Secondly, they plan to deliver their first proprietary horizontal plating system for panel-level packaging. Panel-level packaging is a major growth frontier for advanced AI chips. If they announce repeat orders from global tier 1 fabs for this technology, the market will have to price them as a global innovator rather than just a Chinese localisation play

Finally, the company is accelerating investments in its Oregon facility to support domestic US semiconductor production. Winning business from a major US fab like Intel would help counter the geopolitical risk narrative and force a re-rating.

Unique Risks

The most obvious risk to the thesis is international trade policy. Management openly states their financial outlook relies on their assessment of international trade policies and supply chain constraints. If the US imposes a blanket ban on all tool sales or servicing to Chinese fabs, ACMR would be caught in the crossfire regardless of their local manufacturing footprint.

Customer concentration is the other key risk factor. The Chinese memory and logic champions like YMTC and SMIC are spending heavily right now. If those companies suddenly cut their capital expenditure budgets due to overcapacity or macro weakness, ACMR's revenue growth would stall. You are betting that the Chinese state mandate for semiconductor self-sufficiency outweighs traditional economic cycles.

Bottom Line

The easy money in ACM Research was made between $32 and $65. The market correctly identified that pricing a rapidly growing, highly profitable company at a massive discount to its own subsidiary was a mistake.

However, in my view, the current valuation still does not reflect the structural shift in the business. They are heavily capitalised, guiding for aggressive 2026 growth, and rolling out tools that command higher sale prices. Between $90 and $133, the company is valued in line with its own subsidiary or by applying a reasonable EV/sales multiple. The holding company discount provides a margin of safety, while the AI-driven localisation wave in China provides the growth. I believe the re-rating is only halfway done.

data.title: The Rise And Fall Of The Silver Price
data.description:
Originally published on February 12, 2026

The silver price trajectory in January was like a shooting star - a bright but short-lived journey from the first to the penultimate trading day of the month. The London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) price climbed 60% since Jan. 2 to reach its all-time high of $118.45 per ounce before dropping 13% day over day and another 21% to $81.975/oz on Feb. 2.

The recent rise and fall of the silver price was caused by economic hedging and supportive fundamentals in a frantic bout of short positioning undone by the intense pressure of CME Group margin hikes. Some will recall the "speculative blow-off top" followed by a "sudden reality check" associated with the Hunt Brothers events of 1980.

The rise

Like gold, silver has intrinsic value that investors seek in times of economic and geopolitical turmoil, yet the market is simultaneously quieter and more volatile. It is smaller, somewhat illiquid and with a lower price tag - all the makings of a highly leveraged commodity.

Ongoing geopolitical tensions, reduced expectations for global trade growth and some protection over currency debasement (especially with respect to a weakening US dollar amid rising debt, economic tensions as well as the anticipation of weaker Treasury bond yields) have been supporting the silver price, echoing the dynamics for gold.

In addition, silver has industrial applications that account for approximately half of the demand. This proportion could rise with the clean energy push and AI revolution, as silver is widely used in solar panels, electric vehicles and electronics, particularly for data centers.

Fundamentals are also supportive of the silver price: the market has been in deficit, in part due to silver generally being a byproduct and therefore depending on other commodities' mined output.

The supply deficit also meant that most of the "paper" silver traded on stock exchanges - i.e., futures contracts - typically outvalues the amount of silver metal available, and even more so during this bull run. Tight supply, strong dual precious and industrial demand, and a great deal of speculation drove the silver price to an all-time high.

The fall

The first crack came in mid-January on the back of a physical run, as institutional investors pulled up their future deliveries, withdrawing about a quarter of COMEX's silver inventory. This event deepened the backwardation of the futures curve that started in late 2025, signaling increased scarcity and fueling the price rise.

The higher leverage caused COMEX owner CME Group Inc. to implement a percentage-based margin system at the exchange to keep the market in check. CME raised the silver margin requirement a few times, with a Jan. 30 emergency hike causing a massive sell-off and triggering a bear market in two consecutive sessions.

Despite the simultaneous announcement of a new, hawkish US Federal Reserve Chair, the crash was a classic response mechanism within futures trading. CME Group's actions should have been anticipated given the extreme market volatility, but they do not predict any subsequent price trajectories.

However, the recent events indicate overall market resilience as demand is supported on two grounds. The first is the current global picture, with geopolitical instability, economic uncertainty and growing industrial demand amid a recurring supply deficit. The second is past experience, with the 2011 margin hikes merely having halted a price rise.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Q4 2025 U.S. Retail Preview: Leisure Products, Hotels And Restaurants Outperform
data.description:
By Jharonne Martis

The LSEG U.S. Retail and Restaurant Q4 2025 earnings index, which tracks changes in the growth rate of earnings within the sector, is expected to show a 2.2% growth over last year’s levels. Our metrics show that five of 10 consumer-related industries have turned negative (Exhibit 1).

Of the 190 retailers tracked by LSEG, the leisure products sector is headed for the highest earnings growth rate in the fourth quarter, recording an estimated 46.8% surge over last year’s level. The second-strongest sector is Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure, with an 18.8% growth estimate.

At the other end of the spectrum, Household Durables has the weakest anticipated Q4 2025 estimate, with profits expected to decline by -28.7% (Exhibit 1). To date, 90 of the 190 companies in our Retail/Restaurant Index have reported their EPS results for Q4 2025, representing 47% of the index.

Exhibit 1: The LSEG Retail Earnings Growth Rate – Q4 2025

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

The strongest sector is the Leisure Products group. Of the seven companies in this group, four are on track to post positive estimated earnings growth for Q4. Hasbro and Brunswick reported the strongest earnings growth rates of 228.0%, and 141.7%, respectively. Similarly, Callaway Golf (CALY) and Mattel (MAT) reported robust earnings growth rates of 24.2% and 11.4%, respectively.

The second strongest sector is the Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure sector. Within this group, United Natural Foods (UNFI) is on track to post the strongest estimated earnings growth rate at 156.3%. In fact, of the 51 companies in this category, only 17 are expected to report negative earnings growth for Q4. Caesars Entertainment (CZR) and Cracker Barrel (CBRL) are projected to deliver weak results, with negative estimated earnings growth rates of -429.5% and -122.5%, respectively.

Meanwhile, the Household Durables group is on track to post the weakest year-over-year earnings comparisons. Negative growth expectations are directly responsible for the forecast decline in the overall earnings growth rate within the group. Twenty-one out of 27 companies struggled to match year-ago earnings growth levels. Whirlpool Corp. (WHR) already reported a 75.9% decline in earnings, while LGI Homes (LGIH) is on track to report -55.3% decline in earnings growth in the third quarter of 2025.

So far, 90 companies or 47.4% of those in our Retail/Restaurant Index, have reported earnings for Q4 2025. Of this group, 60% announced earnings that exceeded analysts’ expectations, 7% matched and the remaining 33% reported earnings that fell below analysts’ predictions (Exhibit 2). The blended earnings growth estimate for Q4 2025 is 2.2%.

To date, 90 companies in the Retail/Restaurant index have reported revenue for Q4 2025. For this group, the Q4 2025 blended revenue growth estimate is 5.1%; 71% have reported revenue above analyst expectations, and 29% reported revenue below analyst expectations.

Exhibit 2: LSEG Earnings Dashboard

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

Guidance

To date, 90 retailers have reported Q4 2025 earnings, with many citing elevated prices, persistent macroeconomic challenges, and a more cautious consumer as key headwinds. Additionally, roughly half of these companies flagged tariffs as a drag on performance this quarter.

The bulk of retailers still have to report Q4 2025 results. Going into the quarter, 26 retailers issued negative preannouncements, while 15 issued positive EPS guidance for Q4 2025 so far (Exhibit 3). Of those retailers offering revenue guidance, 31 warned of disappointing results, while 19 said revenue might be better than previously expected in Q4 2025.

Looking forward to Q1 2026, 12 retailers issued negative earnings preannouncements, while only seven issued positive EPS guidance for Q1 2026. Of those retailers offering revenue guidance, eight warned of disappointing results, while four said revenue might be better than previously expected in Q1 2026.

Exhibit 3: Earnings and Revenue Guidance: Q4 2025 – Q1 2026

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

Retail sales

The LSEG Same Store Sales (SSS) index is expected to see a robust 4.8% gain in Q4 2025 (Exhibit 4). An increase of 3.0% in SSS signals that consumer spending is healthy. Looking back one year, Q4 2024 SSS notched a gain of 4.7% which makes this quarter’s estimate especially noteworthy.

It’s very important to note that due to the pandemic, the 2020-2022 results don’t offer an apples-to-apples comparison of current trends relative to previous years, as many retailers were closed due to shelter in place regulations.

Exhibit 4: LSEG Same Store Sales Index: 2021 – Present

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

When it comes to apparel, retailers that offer shoppers a steady stream of novelty tend to cultivate strong customer loyalty. Aritzia (ATZAF), in particular, beat its 19.5% SSS estimate and delivered the strongest same-store sales (SSS) result of the quarter, with an increase of 34.3%. Notably, seven of the top ten SSS performers this quarter are in the apparel category.

Among the standouts, Genesco (GCO) and American Eagle (AEO) are expected to post SSS growth of 9.0% and 8.9%, respectively. Likewise, Anthropologie and Free People are driving a projected 5.1% gain for their parent company, Urban Outfitters (URBN).

At the same time, consumers continue to feel the pressure of elevated food prices, sustaining demand for discount retailers. These value-driven stores remain resilient, holding steady business volumes despite challenging year-over-year comparisons.

Walmart (WMT), known for its strong value proposition and loyal customer base, is expected to post a 4.2% SSS increase. Meanwhile, Costco (COST) already reported a robust 6.4% gain, despite facing tough comparisons from a year ago.

Exhibit 5: Strongest Same Store Sales Estimates: Q4 2025 Estimate vs. Q4 2024 Actual

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

On the other hand, mall-based and big-box department stores continue to fall out of favor with consumers. Target (TGT) and Kohl’s (KSS) are projected to report weak Q4 2025 same-store sales (SSS) declines of -2.3% and -1.6%, respectively (see Exhibit 6). Other underperformers such as J. Jill (JILL), Kirkland, and Shoe Carnival (SCVL) remain in the bottom tier as they grapple with ongoing company-specific challenges.

Exhibit 6: Weakest Same Store Sales Estimates: Q4 2025 Estimate vs. Q4 2024 Actual

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

Restaurant Same Store Sales

The LSEG Restaurant Same Store Sales (SSS) index is expected to see a 1.9% growth in SSS in Q4 2025, on top of facing last year’s easy comparison of -1.3%. (Exhibit 7).

Within this industry, the Quick Service sector is on top with a 2.0% SSS estimate, stronger than the Casual Dining sector. The Casual Dining sector is on track to see a 1.7% SSS.

It’s important to note that, once again, the 2020-2022 results don’t offer an apples-to-apples comparison over previous years, given that quarantine rules and other pandemic restrictions forced many restaurants to close. As a result, a number of restaurants didn’t report SSS data during the pandemic.

Exhibit 7: LSEG Restaurant Same Store Sales Index: 2021 – Present

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

In the restaurant sector, approximately 68% of the companies in our same-store sales (SSS) index have reported or are on track to report positive Q4 2025 results. However, it’s important to note that many are facing difficult year-over-year comparisons. On the weaker end, CBRL Group has one of the lowest estimates at -8.4%, while Wingstop (WING) is expected to report a -6.6% decline as it is facing one of the toughest comparisons from a year ago with a 10.1% gain.

Exhibit 8: Weakest Restaurant Same Store Sales Estimates: Q4 2025 Estimate vs. Q4 2024 Actual

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

On the positive side, there are notable standouts. Brinker International (EAT) already reported a 7.5% SSS, surpassing its 5.6% estimate and last year’s 7.5% growth. Similarly, Darden (DRI) and Yum Brands (YUM) already beat their SSS estimates and delivered solid comps of 4.3% and 3.0%, respectively.

Exhibit 9: Strongest Restaurant Same Store Sales Estimates: Q4 2025 Estimate vs. Q4 2024 Actual

Source: LSEG I/B/E/S

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
