data.title: Hermes: At This Point, Its Resilience Is Ridiculous
data.description:
AI, geopolitics, tariffs, China, Taiwan, and foreign currency—they cause volatility and disruption to everybody, but not Hermes (HESAF) (HESAY).

The French conglomerate seems to be operating in a different world, with results that exceed the industry and expectations time and time again.

That said, as other stocks sell off and become increasingly attractive, Hermes' relative valuation becomes even more prohibitive. Shares remain a "Hold."

There's Hermes, Then There's Everybody Else

Looking back to 2025, it can be broadly characterized as a year of extreme uncertainty for retail companies, specifically around tariffs and macro pressures.

In light of such a year, Hermes results shine even brighter. Revenues grew 5.4% on a reported basis but 9% at constant rates. Despite the FX headwinds, Hermes was able to improve its margins, with gross margins expanding 80 bps and operating margins expanding 50 bps.

Besides operational efficiencies, the improvements in margins were supported by a better mix across geographies and segments. In 2025, growth was led by the Middle East, Japan, Americas, and Europe, countries with relatively higher margins.

Further, there was a slight change in mix towards more profitable product categories, as Leather Goods & Saddlery gained 1 point, and Other Hermes Sectors gained 1 point as well.

The results in 2025 create a very strong setup for 2026. I expect to see at least stable margins at these record levels, alongside stable or accelerating growth. This says a lot at a time when some peers are still struggling to return to growth.

Another Year Of Industry Outperformance

In the past, investors, including myself, used to debate about which luxury stock was more attractive. There were comparisons drawn between Hermes, Ferrari (RACE), LVMH (LVMHF), Kering (PPRUF), and others.

Most of the time, the conclusion was that Hermes is likely the strongest fundamentally, but that relative to its premium valuation, the answer wasn't always clear.

Well, one thing I know for sure is that I don't see myself investing in the so-called Hermes competitors in the near future.

As we can see, it's difficult to find a single quarter where they come even close to Hermes on an organic growth basis.

The same is true if we look at specific product categories, as Hermes has been outperforming on Leather, its main segment, every quarter since I started my tracking.

I've learned that in the majority of cases, you're better off investing in the industry leader, regardless of valuation, than you are investing in the laggards just because they are cheaply valued.

Indeed, if you look at Hermes' performance relative to other luxury names, the evidence is clear.

So, in my view, it's either Hermes or nothing when it comes to this sector. The issue is that its industry leadership is far from being a secret.

But Resiliency Isn't Being Translated To The Stock's Performance

Hermes delivered consistently impressive results over the past few years, with a sales CAGR of 12.7% over 10 years, and a net income CAGR of 16.6%. Over the past four years, sales rose 38%, and net income grew over 44%.

Despite the extraordinary performance, the stock has been a significant underperformer over the past three years, due to a combination of very high valuation, decelerating growth, and stable margins.

Looking ahead, the question that investors should ask is whether the current setup is any different. I argue that it isn't.

As we'll discuss, Hermes still trades at a high valuation. Growth is expected to continue at current levels, in the range of 8%-9%, and margins aren't expected to expand from current record levels.

So, in my view, as impressive as results are, it's hard to find a bull case here.

Valuation Becomes Increasingly Prohibitive

At $2,572 per ADR, and 2,066 euros per share, Hermes trades around 45 times forward earnings, roughly in line with its historical levels.

This puts Hermes at the usual premium over luxury peers like LVMH and Richemont. It also places Hermes at over a 2x premium vs. the S&P 500.

Meanwhile, Hermes is expected to grow revenues by about 9% this year, and EPS at a similar pace. Both are lower than the market average.

So, in simple words, you're getting one of the most resilient businesses (if not the most), but it comes at a very lofty price.

I don't think it's reasonable to assume multiple expansion going forward, so at best, the projected returns are in line with EPS growth, plus dividends.

With an EPS growth trajectory of 8%-9% and a 0.9% dividend yield, we're looking at less than 10% annual returns.

Now, the issue is that even the slightest multiple expansion could take away a full year of gains. And let me ask you the following: Would it be so wild if Hermes traded at 40 times earnings instead of 45? I think the answer is no.

Conclusion

Hermes continues to live in a different sphere, where it appears nothing whatsoever can prevent the stock from maintaining high-single-digit organic growth, with extraordinary margins.

However, investing isn't a resiliency race; it's about finding attractively valued businesses that are being underappreciated by the market. Hermes would win such a race, but I don't find it meets the criteria for being a successful investment.

As long as the valuation premium remains at extreme levels of more than 2.5x the market average, a "Hold" remains the right rating, in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Compelling Value Proposition, But Risks Greatly Elevated
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from this fund letter.

The main hit to the fund this year was UnitedHealth (UNH), impacting performance by -1.1%, when health costs for its enrollees surged unexpectedly.

UnitedHealth had a disastrous 2025. The year began with a leak in February that the Department of Justice had launched an investigation into the company’s Medicare Advantage ('MA') coding practices. In March, it actually won an MA coding case dating back to the Obama Administration, but it was a good reminder that investigations are easier to start than to win.

In April, the floor gave way. UnitedHealth announced that its membership, both on the insurance side and on its Optum healthcare services side, turned out to be far sicker and more expensive to treat than the firm had anticipated. The health insurance business is a low margin business that depends on actuarial accuracy: tiny changes in medical loss ratio have a tremendous impact on profitability. In May, UnitedHealth replaced then-CEO Andrew Witty with former CEO Stephen Hemsley, which confirmed that the errors were not the result of a fluke month of bad luck, but reflected poor modeling that would take some time to resolve. By August, the stock that had begun the year at $510 was trading below $240. Hemsley focused on shedding unprofitable customers and righting the ship, and while he said it would take much of 2026 to get back on track, the earnings potential of the franchise was compelling.

We try to limit our discussion to events that happened during the period in question, but it is hard to discuss UnitedHealth without mentioning that in January 2026 the government issued its advance notice for MA rates in 2027. While the government acknowledged a 4.97% growth rate in costs, it offset this increase with other changes in its model that yielded a net change of only 0.09%.

This is a proposal. The final rate determination will not take place until April or be effective until October. Quite a bit of back and forth will happen between now and then. That said, we are clear-eyed that it is not sustainable to run a business with costs that increase 5% and revenue that remains flat. MA is a wildly popular program. The majority of Medicare beneficiaries enroll in it, about 35 million Americans, and draconian changes to their plans won’t go unnoticed. We believe UnitedHealth offers a compelling value proposition and, given its numerous advantages, will eventually manage through this, but we are aware that the risks are greatly elevated.

The fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses must be considered carefully before investing. The prospectus contains this and other important information about the investment company, and it may be obtained here or by calling 800.231.2901. Read it carefully before investing.

An investment in the fund is subject to investment risks, including the possible loss of the principal amount invested. There can be no assurance that the fund will be successful in meeting its objectives. The fund invests in common stocks which subjects investors to market risk. The fund invests in small and micro-cap companies, which involve additional risks such as limited liquidity and greater volatility. The fund invests in undervalued securities. Undervalued securities are, by definition, out of favor with investors, and there is no way to predict when, if ever, the securities may return to favor. The fund invests in foreign securities which involve greater volatility and political, economic and currency risks and differences in accounting methods. Investments in debt securities typically decrease in value when interest rates rise. This risk is usually greater for longer-term debt securities. More information about these risks and other risks can be found in the fund’s prospectus. The fund is a nondiversified fund and therefore may be subject to greater volatility than a more diversified investment.
